claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 14 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
circumstances: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to understand your situation. 

**First question: How old is your child (or children)?**

The age of your child is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** typically don't qualify

Please tell me the age(s) of the child/children you're claiming for, and their name(s) so I can help assess each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi, yes I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and wanted to check if I'm eligible.

I have two children - Alex who is 14, and Blake who is 19.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you check eligibility for both Alex (14) and Blake (19).

Let me start with **Alex, who is 14 years old**. Since Alex is under 16, let me check the next requirement:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (14 years old):**

Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?

Once you answer this, I'll also need to assess Blake (19 years old) separately, as the requirements differ for children aged 16-19.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - they're only 14, still in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Alex is still in school and not working, let me continue with the next check:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (14 years old):**

Is Alex receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

(Most 14-year-olds don't receive these benefits, but I need to confirm this to proceed.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't get any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue checking Alex's eligibility:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (14 years old):**

Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program? (This would include apprenticeships in England, Scotland, or Wales - though most 14-year-olds in regular school are not in apprenticeships.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in an apprenticeship - they're just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me move to the next check:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status as the claimant:

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **ANY** of the following applies to you:

1. You normally live in the UK
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively seeking work (for up to 91 days)
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support your family
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

Which of these situations applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the next check:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex (14 years old):**

Are you responsible for Alex? You meet this requirement if **ANY** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex**
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care - this can include food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement that equals or exceeds Child Benefit payments

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me - they live with my ex-partner. I don't currently contribute financially to Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. This is important information. If you don't live with Alex and don't contribute financially to their upkeep, then you wouldn't meet the responsibility requirement for Alex.

Let me now assess **Blake (19 years old)** before we determine the overall outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (19 years old):**

Since Blake is 19, they may still qualify if they're in approved education or training. Let me check:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (19 years old):**

Which of the following applies to Blake:

1. **Currently in education or training**
2. **Recently left education** (within the last 20 weeks)
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and didn't recently leave

What's Blake's current situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake is currently in education - doing A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me check if Blake's A-levels qualify as approved education:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! A-levels are approved education. Now I need to check a few more details about Blake's A-level course:

**For Blake (19 years old):**

1. Is Blake studying **full-time** (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)?
2. Is this education **NOT funded by an employer** or leading to a job agreed with an employer?

Please confirm these details about Blake's A-level course.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is studying full-time - it's a regular sixth form college course, not funded by any employer.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue checking Blake's eligibility:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (19 years old):**

Is Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work - just focuses on the studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (19 years old):**

Is Blake receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Let me continue:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (19 years old):**

Just to confirm - Blake is doing A-levels at sixth form, not an apprenticeship, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, just regular A-levels at sixth form college, not an apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: We've already confirmed you live in the UK (Manchester), so that requirement is met. Now let me check the responsibility requirement for Blake:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (19 years old):**

Are you responsible for Blake? You meet this requirement if **ANY** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Blake**
2. **You contribute to Blake's upkeep** (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care - this can include food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents)
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement that equals or exceeds Child Benefit payments

Do any of these apply to your situation with Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue checking Blake's eligibility:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (19 years old):**

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake, or planning to claim?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, my ex-partner already claims for Blake actually.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see - this is an important situation. Let me ask:

**Is your ex-partner willing to transfer the claim to you, or is there a dispute about who should be claiming Child Benefit for Blake?**

The answer depends on whether:
- You and your ex-partner **agree** that the claim should transfer to you, OR
- There's a **dispute** - you both want to claim and can't agree on who should receive it

What's the situation between you and your ex-partner regarding Blake's Child Benefit claim?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: We haven't really discussed it - they're already claiming it and Blake lives with them most of the time I suppose. I mean, Blake lives with me, but my ex-partner has been the one claiming.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I need to clarify Blake's living situation, as this is crucial:

You mentioned earlier that **Blake lives at home with you**, but now you're saying **Blake lives with your ex-partner most of the time**. 

Can you clarify:
1. **Where does Blake primarily live?** (With you, with your ex-partner, or split between both homes?)
2. If Blake splits time between homes, approximately how much time is spent at each home?

This is important because the person the child lives with typically has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Sorry, let me be clear - Blake does live with me. My ex-partner is the one who's been claiming the Child Benefit though.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So Blake lives with you, but your ex-partner is currently claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

This creates a situation where there are potentially two claimants. Let me ask:

**Have you and your ex-partner discussed or agreed on who should be claiming Child Benefit for Blake?**

- If you both **agree** that you should claim (and your ex-partner would stop their claim), we can proceed
- If there's **no agreement or a dispute** about who should claim, HMRC would need to decide

What's the situation - is there agreement, or would this be disputed?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: We haven't agreed anything - they're claiming it and I suppose there would be a dispute if I tried to claim it as well.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand. Since there's a potential dispute, let me navigate to the appropriate outcome:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Now let me provide you with the complete assessment for both children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Summary

Based on the information you've provided, here's the outcome for each of your children:

---

### **Alex (14 years old) - NOT ELIGIBLE**

❌ You cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex because you don't meet the **responsibility requirement**.

**Why:** Although Alex meets all the basic qualifying criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship), you must either:
- Live with the child, OR
- Contribute financially to their upkeep (at least the equivalent of Child Benefit)

Since Alex lives with your ex-partner and you don't currently contribute to their upkeep, you cannot claim for Alex.

**What you could do:** If you wanted to claim for Alex in the future, you would need to either have Alex live with you, or start making regular financial contributions towards their care that equal or exceed Child Benefit payments.

---

### **Blake (19 years old) - DEFERRED TO HMRC**

⚠️ Blake qualifies as an eligible child, and you meet most requirements, BUT there's a **disputed multiple claimant situation**.

**Why Blake qualifies:**
- Blake is 19 and in approved full-time education (A-levels)
- Not working or receiving benefits
- Lives with you (meeting the responsibility requirement)
- You live in the UK

**The issue:** Your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and you indicated there would be a dispute if you tried to claim. When two people cannot agree who should claim, **HMRC must make the decision**.

**What HMRC considers:**
1. Who the child lives with most of the time (primary residence)
2. Who contributes most to the child's upkeep
3. The child's best interests
4. Continuity of existing arrangements

**Your next steps for Blake:**
- Contact the Child Benefit Office and explain that Blake lives with you
- Provide evidence of Blake's living arrangements (e.g., school records showing your address, medical records, etc.)
- HMRC will contact both you and your ex-partner to gather information
- HMRC's decision will be binding

📞 **Child Benefit Office:** 0300 200 3100  
🔗 **More information:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit-child-lives-with-someone-else

---

### **Summary**

You currently cannot claim Child Benefit for either child, but for different reasons:
- **Alex:** You don't meet the responsibility requirement
- **Blake:** HMRC needs to resolve the competing claims between you and your ex-partner

If Blake truly lives with you primarily, you have a strong case to make to HMRC, and I'd encourage you to contact them with evidence of the living arrangements.

reasonings: Alex is 14 years old and meets the basic qualifying child criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship). However, you are NOT ELIGIBLE to claim Child Benefit for Alex because you do not meet the responsibility requirement. Alex lives with your ex-partner, and you do not contribute financially to Alex's upkeep. Under Child Benefit rules, you must either live with the child OR contribute at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care. Since neither applies, you cannot claim for Alex.

Blake is 19 years old and meets the qualifying child criteria (in approved full-time education doing A-levels, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship). You meet the responsibility requirement as Blake lives with you, and you meet the UK residency requirement. However, your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and you indicated there would be a dispute if you tried to claim. This is a DEFERRED situation - HMRC must decide between competing claimants. HMRC will consider who Blake lives with most of the time, who contributes most to their upkeep, and Blake's best interests. You should contact the Child Benefit Office with evidence that Blake lives with you.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You have two children: Alex (14) and Blake (19). For Alex, you are not eligible because you don't meet the responsibility requirement - Alex lives with your ex-partner and you don't contribute to their upkeep. For Blake, while Blake lives with you and meets all qualifying criteria, your ex-partner is already claiming, creating a disputed multiple claimant situation that HMRC must resolve.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.reason: Multiple people claim responsibility for the child and cannot agree who should claim Child Benefit
response.guidance: When two or more people are responsible for a child and cannot agree who should claim, HMRC will make the decision. HMRC considers: (1) Who the child lives with most of the time (primary residence), (2) Who contributes most to the child's upkeep, (3) The child's best interests, (4) Continuity of existing arrangements. Both parties should contact the Child Benefit Office with evidence of their circumstances. HMRC's decision is binding. Common scenarios: separated parents with shared custody, child living part-time with each parent, grandparent vs parent both contributing.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 14 years old and meets the basic qualifying child criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship). However, you are NOT ELIGIBLE to claim Child Benefit for Alex because you do not meet the responsibility requirement. Alex lives with your ex-partner, and you do not contribute financially to Alex's upkeep. Under Child Benefit rules, you must either live with the child OR contribute at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care. Since neither applies, you cannot claim for Alex.

Blake is 19 years old and meets the qualifying child criteria (in approved full-time education doing A-levels, not working, not receiving benefits, not in an apprenticeship). You meet the responsibility requirement as Blake lives with you, and you meet the UK residency requirement. However, your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and you indicated there would be a dispute if you tried to claim. This is a DEFERRED situation - HMRC must decide between competing claimants. HMRC will consider who Blake lives with most of the time, who contributes most to their upkeep, and Blake's best interests. You should contact the Child Benefit Office with evidence that Blake lives with you.

response.overall_reasoning: You have two children: Alex (14) and Blake (19). For Alex, you are not eligible because you don't meet the responsibility requirement - Alex lives with your ex-partner and you don't contribute to their upkeep. For Blake, while Blake lives with you and meets all qualifying criteria, your ex-partner is already claiming, creating a disputed multiple claimant situation that HMRC must resolve.
